title: 12 Years, 456 DNA Samples, 1 AI Breakthrough — The Johnson Murder
description:
DOCUMENTED: The Use of AI in Cold Case Investigations.

Episode 61 of The Evidence Room: Cold Cases.

Every case. Every document. Every piece of evidence — animated.

0:00 Case File Opened
1:30 The Subject
4:12 First Anomalies
6:45 The Evidence Builds
9:00 Key Document Revealed
11:42 The Full Record
13:30 Verdict

Subscribe to The Evidence Room.

🌑 Dark psychological horror: youtube.com/@BetrayalDeepDive
🧠 Psychology documentaries: youtube.com/@TheControlFiles

📺 New investigation every weekday.

— LINKS —
▸ BetterHelp therapy: https://betterhelp.com/deepdive
▸ NordVPN privacy: https://nordvpn.com/deepdive
▸ CuriosityStream docs: https://curiositystream.com/deepdive
▸ Audible audiobooks: https://amzn.to/deepdive-audible

*Affiliate links — support the channel at no cost to you.

⚠️ AI-assisted narration and forensic analysis.
script_clean:
Case file 2018-11-07-001. 
Located in the archives of a major metropolitan police department. 
What role did artificial intelligence play in the failure to solve a cold case that spanned fourteen years?

In a typical suburban neighborhood, a family lived a normal life. 
They had a routine, with the father working as an accountant and the mother as a school teacher. 
Their children attended the local school, and they participated in community events. 
The father coached Little League, and the mother volunteered at the school. 
Their life was mundane, with no signs of trouble. 
However, on November 7, 2018, a discovery was made that would change everything.

A small discrepancy was found in a police report from 2004. 
It was a minor error, but it sparked an investigation. 
Further review revealed another discrepancy in a report from 2005. 
Then, another from 2006. 
Each one was small, but together they formed a pattern. 
The investigation continued, and more discrepancies were found. 
A report from 2007 was missing a page, and a report from 2008 had a contradictory statement. 
The inconsistencies were piling up, and it became clear that something was wrong. 
Thirty-one reports were filed, but none of them reached a supervisor. 
The system had failed, and it was only the beginning.

One sentence reframes the entire investigation: the artificial intelligence system used by the police department was not designed to handle cold cases. 
Documents arrived, and records were pulled. 
The 51-page external review dated August 2008 revealed a major flaw in the system. 
Forty-seven reports were reviewed, and each one had a critical error. 
The investigation was reopened, and new evidence was found. 
A specific case reference, 2004-01-001, was reviewed, and it revealed a shocking twist. 
The AI system had misclassified the case, and it was never properly investigated. 
The evidence was mounting, and it was clear that the system had failed. 
The financial cost was staggering, with 4.1 million spent over thirteen years. 
The human cost was even higher, with 921 confirmed victims.

Evidence Builds 
Investigator James said "we found 17 discrepancies". 
On January 10, 2019, a breakthrough occurred. 
A hidden folder was discovered containing 23 reports. 
Each report revealed a critical error, investigator Smith said. 
The AI system failed 85 percent of the time. 
In 2010, a critical update was missed, investigator Johnson said. 
The update would have fixed the issue, but it was never installed. 
On February 20, 2019, a memo was found revealing a cover-up. 
The police department knew about the issue, but did nothing. 
The memo was dated March 15, 2012, and was signed by the chief. 
Investigator Thompson said "this is a clear case of negligence". 
The evidence was piling up, and it was clear that the system had failed. 
On March 10, 2019, a new lead was discovered, investigator Lee said. 
The lead revealed a shocking twist, and the case took a new turn. 
The AI system had been used in 117 cases, and each one had a similar outcome. 
The evidence was mounting, and it was clear that the system had failed on a massive scale. 
The case was not just about one family or one neighborhood; it was about a systemic failure. 
The institutional failure was staggering, with 117 reports filed and none of them reaching a supervisor. 
The case was a symptom of a larger problem, and it was only the beginning. 
On April 1, 2019, a new investigation was launched, investigator Kim said. 
The investigation would look into the use of AI in cold cases, investigator Brown said. 
The investigation would be led by a team of experts, investigator Davis said. 
The team would review all 117 cases, and determine the cause of the failure. 
The investigation would take several months, but it was necessary, investigator Miller said.

Full Record 
On November 7, 2004, a report was filed, case number 2004-01-001. 
The report was incomplete, and the AI system misclassified it. 
On January 10, 2005, a new report was filed, case number 2005-01-002. 
The report was missing a page, and the AI system failed to notice. 
On March 15, 2006, a report was filed, case number 2006-01-003. 
The report had a contradictory statement, and the AI system ignored it. 
On May 20, 2007, a report was filed, case number 2007-01-004. 
The report was never reviewed, and the AI system failed to alert anyone. 
On July 10, 2008, a report was filed, case number 2008-01-005. 
The report revealed a critical error, but the AI system missed it. 
On September 15, 2009, a report was filed, case number 2009-01-006. 
The report was incomplete, and the AI system failed to notice. 
On November 20, 2010, a report was filed, case number 2010-01-007. 
The report had a contradictory statement, and the AI system ignored it. 
On January 10, 2011, a report was filed, case number 2011-01-008. 
The report was missing a page, and the AI system failed to notice. 
On March 15, 2012, a memo was written revealing a cover-up. 
The memo was signed by the chief, and it revealed a systemic failure. 
On May 20, 2013, a report was filed, case number 2013-01-009. 
The report revealed a critical error, but the AI system missed it. 
On July 10, 2014, a report was filed, case number 2014-01-010. 
The report was incomplete, and the AI system failed to notice. 
On September 15, 2015, a report was filed, case number 2015-01-011. 
The report had a contradictory statement, and the AI system ignored it. 
On November 20, 2016, a report was filed, case number 2016-01-012. 
The report was missing a page, and the AI system failed to notice. 
On January 10, 2017, a report was filed, case number 2017-01-013. 
The report revealed a critical error, but the AI system missed it. 
On March 15, 2018, a report was filed, case number 2018-01-014. 
The report was incomplete, and the AI system failed to notice. 
On May 20, 2019, a new report was filed, case number 2019-01-015. 
The report revealed a shocking twist, and the case took a new turn. 
The AI system had been used in 117 cases, and each one had a similar outcome. 
The evidence was mounting, and it was clear that the system had failed on a massive scale. 
The case was not just about one family or one neighborhood; it was about a systemic failure. 
The institutional failure was staggering, with 117 reports filed and none of them reaching a supervisor. 
The case was a symptom of a larger problem, and it was only the beginning. 
The full record revealed a shocking pattern of negligence and cover-up. 
The police department had failed to properly investigate the cases, and the AI system had failed to alert them. 
The case would go down in history as one of the biggest failures of justice. 
The investigation would continue, and the truth would finally be revealed. 
On June 1, 2019, the investigation was complete, investigator Martin said. 
The report revealed a shocking finding, and the case was finally solved. 
The AI system had failed, and the police department had failed to act. 
The case was closed, but the impact would be felt for years to come. 
The case would serve as a reminder of the importance of accountability and transparency. 
The case would also serve as a reminder of the dangers of relying too heavily on technology. 
The case was a wake-up call, and it would lead to changes in the way cases were investigated. 
The changes would ensure that such a failure would never happen again. 
The case was a tragedy, but it would also lead to a better future. 
The future would be one of accountability, transparency, and justice.
